title: A Train Through the Countryside
author: Sleep Reader
paragraphs: You are sitting by the window of a slow train, the kind that stops at every small station and is in no hurry to reach its destination. The seat beneath you is worn velvet, soft and warm, and it holds you the way a favorite armchair does. Your bag is on the rack above, and your coat is folded beside you, and there is nothing you need to do for the next several hours except sit here and watch the world pass by. The train rocks gently, side to side, a rhythm as old and steady as a cradle.

Outside the window, the countryside unrolls like a bolt of green cloth. The fields are wide and flat, divided by low hedgerows and the occasional line of poplars standing tall and thin like sentries. The grass has that particular shade of green that only exists in late afternoon, when the light comes in at an angle and turns everything gold at the edges. A farmhouse sits in the distance, its chimney producing a thin ribbon of smoke that leans sideways in the breeze and then dissolves.

The train passes a small pond where a heron stands motionless in the shallows, its grey body a perfect mirror of patience. You wonder how long it has been standing there, waiting for a fish or perhaps not waiting for anything at all, simply being a heron in a pond on a quiet afternoon. The scene slides past the window and is replaced by a row of apple trees, their branches heavy with fruit that catches the light like small lanterns.

A village appears and disappears in the space of a minute. You glimpse a church steeple, a row of stone cottages with blue and red doors, a woman hanging laundry on a line in her garden. The sheets billow in the wind like sails on a landlocked boat. A dog sits on a porch, watching the train with mild interest, its tail offering a single, lazy wag. And then the village is behind you, folded back into the landscape, and the fields return.

The rhythm of the train is a kind of language. The wheels speak to the rails in a repeating phrase, two syllables followed by a pause, like a heartbeat heard through a stethoscope. It enters your body through the soles of your feet and the palms of your hands resting on the armrest, a vibration so steady it becomes invisible, noticed only when it changes. You feel it in your chest, a gentle rocking that says, I am carrying you. You do not need to steer.

A tea trolley comes through the carriage, pushed by a man with kind eyes and an unhurried manner. You take a cup of tea and hold it in both hands, feeling the warmth seep into your fingers. The tea is the color of honey, and it tastes the way comfort feels, familiar, uncomplicated, exactly right. You take a sip and look out the window again. A river has appeared, running alongside the tracks, its surface catching the sky and carrying it downstream in shimmering pieces.

The light is changing. The gold of the afternoon is deepening into amber, and the shadows of the hedgerows are growing long across the fields. A flock of starlings rises from a tree and moves across the sky in a shifting, flowing shape, like a single living thought changing its mind again and again. They settle on a power line and sit in a row, dark commas against the glowing sky, and you wonder what they are discussing.

The train slows as it approaches another small station. The platform is empty except for a bench and a hanging basket of flowers. No one gets on. No one gets off. The conductor calls the station's name, two soft syllables that sound like a word in a language you almost know, and then the train pulls away again, easing back into its gentle rhythm. The flowers on the platform nod in the wake of the passing carriages.

Your tea is finished, and the cup sits cooling on the small table by the window. Outside, the first lights are appearing in farmhouse windows, warm rectangles of gold scattered across the darkening fields. Each one is a kitchen, a family, a meal being prepared, a day being gently closed. The sight of those lights from a moving train is one of the most peaceful things in the world, proof that everywhere, in houses you will never enter, people are settling in for the evening.

Your eyelids grow heavy. The rocking of the train has found the exact frequency of your tiredness and is amplifying it, not unpleasantly but irresistibly, like a song you cannot help humming along to. The countryside outside is now more shadow than shape, the fields merging into a single dark quilt under the first pale stars. The rhythm of the wheels continues, faithful and unhurried, carrying you onward through the soft, dark land.

You let your head rest against the cool window. The glass hums faintly against your temple. The last thing you see before your eyes close is a single farmhouse light, far away across the fields, glowing like a candle left burning for someone who is almost home. The train rocks you gently, steadily, the way the world rocks everything to sleep when the day is done. Goodnight, traveler. The train knows the way. You can rest now. Sweet dreams.
